texts.text: ABSTRACT

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Nanofiltration (NF) membranes were introduced during the ' 80s with characteristics in between those of reverse osmosis and ultrafiltration membranes. This article aims at critically reviewing advances in NF research including relevance of fabrication techniques, modifications, and current problems. We also haveacomprehensivelookatfoulingmitigationaswell as its prevention by treating NF membranes with functionalized carbon nanotubes. It was found that some parameters such as porosity, crystallinity of polymer and hydrophobicity/hydrophilicity can affect the performance of membranes. NF membranes can be widely utilized in broad spectrum of applications particularly in desalination and wastewater treatment.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The nanofiltration (NF) membrane is a type of pressuredriven membrane having characteristics in between ultrafiltration (UF) and reverse osmosis (RO) membranes. NF gives many benefits that are as high retention of multivalent anion salts and organic molecular above 300, relatively low investment, maintenance costs and low operation, high flux and low operating pressure. These are the benefits which are responsible for increased applications of NF worldwide. [1] The history of NF goes back to the 1970s when RO membranes with a reasonable water flux operating at relatively low pressures were developed. So, in the RO high pressures were used traditionally and resulted in a considerable energy cost. Thus for the separation technology membranes contain lower rejections of dissolved components but with higher water permeability would be a great improvement. Such type of low-pressure RO membranes is represented as NF membranes. [2] The first applications were reported by the second half of the 1980s when NF had been established. [3,4] Hence separation technologies which were using membranes gained interest to be used in purifying macromolecules solutions in lieu of the classical routes [5 -7] with many other techniques being used nowadays in a lot of commercial applications. [8 -12] There are many applications such as stable water filtration characteristics, small system layout requirements, absolute barriers to particles and pathogens, low chemical sludge effluent, compact modular construction, and stationary parts. [13,14] Organic polymeric membranes are advantageous as compared to inorganic membranes due to ease of processing and appropriate robustness. [15] In many different areas, there are many advances over the last 6 years and shown a significant growth of papers published on NF membranes. A total of 1,642 papers have been published on NF membrane based on a quick search using Scopus database from 2008 to present (Figure 1). The main areas of focus in these published papers on NF membranes were in economics and design (0.55%), food applications (2.83%), non-water applications (3.65%), fundamental studies (4%), modeling (7.12%), biotechnology and pharmaceutical (7.49%), desalination (8.95%), fouling (10.68%), miscellaneous topics (11%), membrane fabrication (19%), and environmental applications (25%). The evidence of new applications and research direction were provided by such classification which may assist in understanding the overall view of current research work on NF membrane. Polysulfone silica (Psf-SiO2) and polysulfone graphene oxide (Psf-GO) nanofibers membranes were fabricated by Obaid et al. [16] and investigated high flux water/oil separation. Similarly, the synthesis of polyaniline-based nanomembranes was carried out by Gupta et al. [17] and they performed this process by polymerization of aniline in acidic solution and studies for gas separation applications. Yurekli et al. [18] impregnated zeolite nanoparticles (NPs) in Psf in order to study filtration and adsorption process and for removal of lead and nickel cations. Since the 1960s, with emerging industrial membrane separation processes membrane fouling and its prevention has been an ardent subject of studies. Membrane fouling can be controlled by some factors such as adjustment of operating conditions, selection of appropriate membrane, and appropriate pretreatment of feed solution. Majority of literature addresses the reduction of membrane fouling with organic compounds. It has been shown that resistance of the membrane to organic fouling can be increased by chemical modification of the surface of the membrane with several charged and hydrophilic monomers. The reviews which are previously published discussed to biofouling in membrane system, and they also devoted to addressing their cause, consequences, and control of biofouling including membrane cleaning, biocide application, and feed pretreatment. This mini-review for the first time surveys the studies on categories of polymeric membranes, their fabrication and modification methods, fouling types of fouling and their preventions. Thereafter, the recent studies on the potentiality of carbon nanotubes (CNTs) based membranes are also critically reviewed and lastly, applications of polymeric membranes in numerous fields such as in separation technologies food industries, water treatment in medical applications will be critically summarized.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.3. TR membrane
texts.text: 2.3. TR membrane

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In 2007, Park et al. [25] first proposed the concept of thermally-rearrangedTR polymer. It was considered there are some characteristic features such as wide range of sizes and topologies of free volume elements including pores and channels making it capable to perform molecular separations. The microstructure of polymers was systematically tailored by thermally driven segmental rearrangement. The hydroxyl-containing polyimide precursors and the thermal rearrangement reaction was used for the preparation of TR polymers. The precursor preparation was based on twostep process that contained formation of the hydroxylcontaining poly(amic acids) carried out by thermal imidization. Then, precursor was moved into membrane under argon flow and it was thermally deal under 300 -450°C. Thermally rearranged polybenzoxazole (TRPBO) that is a hollow fiber membrane used for CO2 separation was prepared by Kim et al. [26] from post combustion flue gas. A high CO2 permeance (1940 gas permeation unit (GPU)) was revealed by TR-PBO hollow fiber membranes which were investigated by measuring gas permeation characteristics using constant pressure method with a small laboratory scale test module. The reactions which were involved in the preparation of TR polymers are shown in Figure 2.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.4. Fixed site carrier membrane
texts.text: 2.4. Fixed site carrier membrane

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The Robeson`s upper bound limited the intrinsic permeability and selectivity of polymer membranes. So it was suggested that by including chemical reactions the performance of membranes could be improved which is an excellent alternate option. Fixed site carrier membranes (FSCMs) have been investigated that take advantage of intrinsic chemical reaction with a fixed site. FSCM is a polymeric membrane that has fixed amine-based CO2 carriers in which CO2 reacts with amino groups. The carrier can be both micromolecules like a polymer polyetherimide (PET) and amino acid. Compared with non-reacting materials, the selectivity of FSCM was greatly enhanced owing to chemical reaction. A dense membrane that has selectivity for pure CO2/N2 up to 493 has been reported. According to new research that is performed by Andrew Lee et al. [27] it was discovered that in CO2 capture process some NF membranes from the water treatment have shown good performance. The permeance of CO2 up to 340 GPU and selectivity of CO2/N2 up to 50 was shown by the permeation of humidified gases through the membrane.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.5. Supported ionic liquid membrane
texts.text: 2.5. Supported ionic liquid membrane

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Ionic liquids that are made up of small inorganic anions and large organic cations and near room temperature they are typically viscous liquid. [28] Supported ionic liquid membranes (SILMs) contain porous supports saturated with ionic liquid as a solvent; have the potential for CO2 capturing from flue gas. [29] SILMs and FSCMs both have very high selectivity but under real operating conditions it is difficult to make an assessment of the effective permeance of membranes and their stability over a long time duration is a serious concern. [30] The membrane liquid loss mechanism of SILMs was examined by Zhao et al. [31] under high cross-membrane pressure difference during CO2/N2 separation. It was noted that even serious loss of the membrane liquid might not lead to the chemical deterioration of SILMs which is attributed to the thickness of membrane and diameter of pore which also diminishes with membrane compression and could be calculated by a simple mathematical method. These findings provide a foundational theory for long-term operation of SILMs and an approach to assess the presentation of SILMs.
texts.text: Ionic liquids that are made up of small inorganic anions and large organic cations and near room temperature they are typically viscous liquid. [28] Supported ionic liquid membranes (SILMs) contain porous supports saturated with ionic liquid as a solvent; have the potential for CO2 capturing from flue gas. [29] SILMs and FSCMs both have very high selectivity but under real operating conditions it is difficult to make an assessment of the effective permeance of membranes and their stability over a long time duration is a serious concern. [30] The membrane liquid loss mechanism of SILMs was examined by Zhao et al. [31] under high cross-membrane pressure difference during CO2/N2 separation. It was noted that even serious loss of the membrane liquid might not lead to the chemical deterioration of SILMs which is attributed to the thickness of membrane and diameter of pore which also diminishes with membrane compression and could be calculated by a simple mathematical method. These findings provide a foundational theory for long-term operation of SILMs and an approach to assess the presentation of SILMs.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.1. Interfacial polymerization
texts.text: 4.1. Interfacial polymerization

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The most important method for commercial fabrication of thin film composite (TFC) RO and NF membranes is interfacial polymerization (IP). Cadotte et al. [53] developed first interfacial polymerized TFC membranes and represented a breakthrough in membrane performance for RO applications. [54] The original IP protocol contained an extremely wet microporous polysulfone (PSF) support in an aqueous solution of a polymeric amine followed by submerging the amine saturated membrane into a solution of a diisocyanate in hexane. Afterwards, the membrane was cross-linked by heat treatment at 110°C. [53] The resulting TFC polyurea membrane contained better properties of salt rejection as compared to the integrally skinned asymmetric cellulose acetate membrane and high water flux. [54] A wide variety of TFC membranes have been successfully developed [55,56] because of the significant advantages of IP technique in optimizing the characteristics of skin layer and microporous substrate layer independently. There are various factors that affect the structural morphology and composition of the barrier membrane layer [57 -59] including the concentration of monomers, reaction time, solvent type, and post-treatment conditions.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.2. Phase inversion
texts.text: 4.2. Phase inversion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Phase inversion can be defined as a demixing process where the transformation of initially homogeneous polymer solution takes place in a controlled way from a liquid to a solid state [60] which is achieved in many ways [61] such as (a) Immersion precipitation, (b) Thermally induced phase separation, (c) Evaporation-induced phase separation, and (d) vapor-induced phase separation. In immersion precipitation, the immersion of polymer solution is carried out in a non-solvent coagulation bath typically water. Precipitation and demixing take place owing to exchange of solvent (from polymer solution) and nonsolvent (from coagulation bath), that is the non-solvent and solvent must be miscible. Thermally induced phase separation method based on the phenomenon that with the decrease of temperature, the solvent quality is also normally decreased. The solvent is removed by evaporation, freeze-drying, and extraction after demixing is brought. In evaporation induced phase separation, the polymer solution is prepared in a solvent or in a mixture of non-volatile solvents leading to precipitation/demixing by allowing the solvent to evaporate. This technique is also termed as a solution casting method. In vapor-induced phase separation, the polymer solution is put uncovered to an atmosphere that has a non-solvent (typically water), demixing/precipitation occurs due to the absorption of non-solvent. However, the most commonly used methods among these techniques are thermally induced phase separation and immersion precipitation in the fabrication of polymeric membranes with various methodologies. [62,63]

texts.orig: 4.3. Stretching
texts.text: 4.3. Stretching

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The fabrication of microporous membranes which were mostly used in membrane distillation, microfiltration, and ultra-filtration carried out by extrusion which was followed by stretching technique. In the 1970s the fabrication of polymeric membranes was performed by using stretching owning the companies. Polyethylene (PE) and polypropylene (PP) based membranes are used in energy storage devices [64] and were produced by Celgard. In this solventfree technique, the heating of polymer is carried out above melting temperature and extruded into thin sheets to make it porous by stretching. [65 -67] This technique is particularly useful for the highly crystalline polymers where amorphous regions formed the porous structure and the crystalline regions of the polymer provide strength. There are two steps of stretching; the first step is cold stretching which is followed by the hot stretching. The nucleation of micropores in the precursor film was conceded by cold stretching while to increase/control the final pore structure of membrane it was followed by hot stretching. In this process, the final characteristics and porous structure of the membranes were controlled by the applied processing parameters as well as physical properties of materials including melting point, crystallinity, and tensile strength, etc., [65] The fabrication of PP membrane fabrication was reported by Carreau ' s research group and it was performed by stretching. They also investigated many factors that affect the permeation and morphology. [68 -70]

texts.orig: The use of membrane is limited by a major obstacle termed as fouling which may be present in various forms [101] such as (a) Adsorption: Specific interaction between solute and membrane occurring in the pores or on the surface of membrane. (b) Gel layer formation: Gel layer formation takes place by concentration polarization. This gel layer formation takes place in immediate vicinity of the membrane surface. (c): Pore blackening: The pores of membranes get blocked by the solute resulting in clogging. The fully or partial blackening of pores depends on particle sizes. (d): Cake layer formation: The deposition of particles takes place on the surface of the membrane whose growth occurs layer by layer. texts.orig: It was shown by Baek et al. [104] that the biofouling in a lab-scale cross-flow RO membrane system was not affected by the difference in membrane surface properties like surface charge, hydrophobicity, and roughness. Using three commercial polyamide TFC membranes whose surfaces vary in zeta potential ( -18 to 25 mV), contact angle (31 -44°), and roughness (66 -97 nm). They reported that despite the apparent difference in the surface properties among the selected membranes the extent of biofouling remained constant. It is actually used to prevent biofilm formation incorporating biocides on the membrane surface, which works by killing bacteria which have already attached on a membrane surface and thus it is also needed to prevent their growth. It was concluded having consideration of importance of top layer properties that main aim in developing low bio-fouling composite membrane consisting of hydrophilization, incorporation of charged/bactericide groups, adjusting roughness of surface and polymer brushes on the surface of membranes in order to reduce the undesired interactions with the potential biofoulants to minimize the membrane biofouling. Thus, the main techniques utilized for the development of composite membranes having low fouling characteristics include photon initiated surface grafting, IP, the coating of a thin protective layer, and plasma treatment in addition to the amendment of polymer membranes with NPs (Figure 8). [105] The preparation of a low fouling protective layer can be carried out by layer-by-layer assembly provided that polyelectrolytes were used for modification. It is actually used to prevent biofilm formation incorporating biocides on the membrane surface, which works by killing bacteria which have already attached on a membrane surface and thus it is also needed to prevent their growth. texts.orig: The reduction of membrane biofouling can be carried out by having surface modification using Ag NPs. Using dry jet-wet spinning technique silver-loaded PAN hollow fibers were fabricated, and this preparation was accomplished from a dope containing 0.5 wt.% silver nitrate. [107] It was reported that growth for both Staphylococcus aureus ( S. aureus ) and E. coli was not observed on the surface of the membrane at 0.1 wt.% loading of Ag. The trace amount of silver ions which were released from the fiber is responsible for the antibacterial activity of modified membrane. [108] This antibacterial effect of silver arises from interaction with phosphorous and sulfur of amino acids present in the bacterial cell wall. Such type of interaction occurs via the thiol groups (S-H) which were present in amino acid cysteine. [109] The metabolic activity of bacteria can be altered owing to the existence of such type of interactions prohibiting their growth. [110] The TiO2 NPs significantly affect the degree of fouling mitigation which is located on the surface of membrane exposing to feed solution. In comparison to TiO2 -entrapped membrane the TiO2-deposited membranes showed a higher degree of surface modifications improving fouling mitigation.

texts.orig: In recent years, it is reported that prevention of biofouling and bacterial inactivation can be achieved by the deposition of CNTs onto polymer membranes or porous support layer. The electrically conducting polymer nanocomposites (ECPNC) thin film membranes were fabricated by Lannoy et al. [111] demonstrating biofilm prevention capabilities at high organic and bacterial loadings. Using carboxylated multi-walled CNTs of 10 -30 µm and <8 nm diameter these ECPNC NF membranes were prepared. The deposition of CNT suspension in water along with the incorporation of sodium dodecyl benzene onto PES membrane was performed. This PES support covered with CNT was introduced in an aqueous solution of MPD (2.0% w/w) for 30 s followed by slight drying. Then it was directly immersed in a solution (0.15% w/w) of TMC dispersed in hexane for 30 s. Figure 10 presents the scheme of CNTs incorporation

To achieve a membrane with antifouling characteristics by the incorporation of multiwalled carbon nanotube (MWCNT) in the PES membrane structure was first investigated by Celik et al. [9] The functionalization of MWCNTs was carried out to enhance the diffusion in organic solvents and this functionalization was done with H2SO4/HNO3 solution. Subsequently, flat sheet membrane was produced by blending PES with modified MWCNTs. The hydrogen bonding between carboxylic and sulfonic groups present in MWCNTs and PES confirmed the stability of NPs in polymer matrix. Owing to the presence of MWCNTs,themembranesshowedimprovedperformance indicating membrane roughness, improved hydrophilicity, pure water permeability, and porosity. An improvement in relative flux was obtained by using modified membranes (with 2 wt.% of MWCNT) compared with unmodified membranes and about 42% incremental improvement was achieved. [9]

texts.orig: existing membranes. In separation technology, major problems faced are membrane fouling and pollutant precipitation. Membrane capacities are decreased by irregular pore size, deleterious micropollutants, influent water quality, and P H variations. The precipitation takes place in membranes when water pollutants reach to their solubility limit. In addition, fouling creates defect in membrane pores due to which pores are blocked or membrane regeneration becomes difficult. To overcome the blockages of current separation technologies, MM-CNT membranes could be a great solution. Choi et al. [128] have used MM-CNT membranes to make better the filtration ability of ultra-filtration membranes. It increases water permeability, solutes retention, and mechanical robustness of the membrane. [129] For the separation of inorganic and organic substances in a liquid, NF membrane technology has been developed which work in terms of creating better filtration technologies.

texts.orig: techniques. For the generation of active thin film layer for RO and NF membranes, IP has become very useful as well as practical technique. The formation of a thin layer on the surface of membrane takes place by the reaction and copolymerization of two reactive monomers. In recent decade, the development of TFC membranes through IP has become a matter of interest because it had to improve the membrane characteristics such as selectivity and fouling resistance. This method is easy to apply and reaction is selfinhibiting by providing reactants and by using it a thin film layer can be formed within the 50 nm ranges. The overall efficiency of membranes, retention of solute, and permeability can be determined by this thin active layer which would find out the overall permeability, solute retention, and also the overall efficiency of the particular membrane. Tannic acid, m-phenylenediamine (MPD), Bisphenol A (BPA), and phenyl vinyl amine are various kinds of monomers that have been used in the IP process for the formation of the active thin layer. While on reacting with trimesoyl chloride (TMC) or isophthaloyl chloride these monomers form the thin active film layer.

texts.orig: To obtain P H sensitive membranes the blends of carboxylic polyethersulfone (CPES) and PES were developed by Wang et al. [137] It was found that with increasing concentrations of CPES the contact angle decreases sharply while membrane permeability showed a considerable increase owing to improvement in hydrophilicity resulted from increasing CPES contents. Though the membranes were charged after blending with CPES and the dissociation of carboxylic acid to carboxylate ions impart negative effect on flux of membrane. The pore ring structure membrane is achieved owing to the presence of hydrophilic carboxylic groups in CPES membrane. Another efficient method to make surface hydrophilic and to enhance the resistance to bovine serum albumin is grafting the PEG or pegylating on the backbone of PES. A long term study was conducted by Shi et al. [138] to evaluate the performance of pegylated, unmodified and sulfonated polyether sulfones. SWCNTs constitute single shell of graphene with cylindrical shape while multiple graphene sheets constitute MWCNTs. These SWCNTs and MWCNTs are utilized indirectly to eliminate trouble making compounds complicating the desalination process or directly for water desalination. As CNTs provides a low energy solution for the treatment of water so these are fascinating in advance membrane technologies. The frictionless water flow is provided by CNT membranes retaining water pollutants. The ultra-efficient transport of water molecules takes place owing to smooth hydrophobic walls, high aspect ratio, and inner pore diameter. The spontaneous and uninterrupted flow of water molecules occurs owing to hydrophobic inner core in addition to smooth surface of hollow CNTs (Figure 15) demonstrating two types of CNTs based membranes currently available (1) vertically aligned (VA) (2) mixed matrix (MM) CNT membranes The performance of CNTs based membrane is largely dependent on fabrication and processing technique. texts.label: text
texts.orig: The existence of pollutants such as trace organics and natural organic matter (NOMs) generates a major problem. For the removal of these pollutants, various techniques are employed such as chlorination and coagulation/flocculation. Extra sludge formation takes place by this technology requiring further treatment as well as disposal. In the onset of disease namely Alzheimer, the main role is played by aluminum exposure. Nanotechnology plays an important role in molecular separation technology by providing precise structural controlled material which is mandatory for such necessities. The removal of HA from water is done by using TiO2 nanosized particles which are more prevalent photocatalyst acquiring much attention. This robust TiO2 nanofiber membrane is available in the form of spider web nonwoven and serves as both photocatalysts in water technologies and filtration membrane. It was found that strong oxidant is produced by these crystalline TiO 2 nanofibers in the presence of ultraviolet light which exhibited quantum size effects at nanosize (<10 nm) even this material can be utilized under solar conditions. Taking advantage of such unique characteristics these can be used in making costeffective commercial filtration membranes dramatically reducing the cost of water production. [143] Various membrane methods have been utilized for water treatment purpose which includes UF, microfiltration, NF, membrane distillation, and reverse osmosis. Among these, the well-developed techniques are UF and MF being used for the treatment of water while for water desalination RO is widely used. MD which is new emerging technique has a potential for the desalination of highly saline water. texts.text: The existence of pollutants such as trace organics and natural organic matter (NOMs) generates a major problem. For the removal of these pollutants, various techniques are employed such as chlorination and coagulation/flocculation. Extra sludge formation takes place by this technology requiring further treatment as well as disposal. In the onset of disease namely Alzheimer, the main role is played by aluminum exposure. Nanotechnology plays an important role in molecular separation technology by providing precise structural controlled material which is mandatory for such necessities. The removal of HA from water is done by using TiO2 nanosized particles which are more prevalent photocatalyst acquiring much attention. This robust TiO2 nanofiber membrane is available in the form of spider web nonwoven and serves as both photocatalysts in water technologies and filtration membrane. It was found that strong oxidant is produced by these crystalline TiO 2 nanofibers in the presence of ultraviolet light which exhibited quantum size effects at nanosize (<10 nm) even this material can be utilized under solar conditions. Taking advantage of such unique characteristics these can be used in making costeffective commercial filtration membranes dramatically reducing the cost of water production. texts.orig: photoemission, and catalytic activity NPs are also utilized for the fabrication of NF membranes. Many applications of CNTs based membranes in brackish water desalination and seas are reported in detail while other membranes such as MF, NF, UF, and RO have been used to mitigate freshwater disasters. Thus, membranes constituting CNTs have significant accomplishments in terms of water permeability, solute selectivity, energy savings, robustness, desalination, and antifouling. In general, it can be concluded that NF membranes offer numerous advantages in comparison to conventional once. Fouling is a weakness of NF membranes which is inevitable but it is reversible by using pre-treatment, modification of membrane surface. However, a membrane fouling simulator was developed in order to evaluate fouling potential at an early stage and this was developed by using different chemicals to make NF membrane system less susceptible to fouling. In addition, NF membranes also explored its applications in different fields such as wastewater, pharmaceutical, biotechnology, and in the food industry. We hope the best future regarding CNTs based membranes for desalination and purification in near future.

table_cells.text: UF of BSA solution: Grafting increases the hydrophilicity and antifouling properties of modified membranes [112]
MF of BSA solution: showed better protein resistance after modification and with increase of grafting from 0 to 35.67 wt.% static water contact angle decreases from 145 to 42° [113]
UF of BSA solution: High protein retention low irreversible fouling and high solution flux was shown by the membranes modified with NVP, AMPS, AA. [114]
UF of BSA: showed decreased permeability with increase of grafting but protein rejection remain unaltered after modification [115]
MF of activated sludge: Better filtration performance in the (SMBR) compared with unmodified one and improved antifouling properties [116]
UF of pseudomonas aeruginosa: showed increased fouling as membrane surface more hydrophobic and rough [117]
MF of activated sludge: reduction of water flux was 90.7% after continuous operation of about 70 h while it ranges from 80.8 to 87.2% for modified membrane [118]

UF of homogenized and pasteurized milk with 1.5% fat & 3.2% protein, flux recovery, antifouling properties, flux loss and total flux loss and showed increased fouling resistance due to increased membrane hydrophilicity. [119]
NF of NOM solution: enlargement of pore structure, fouling tendency was decreased for membrane irradiated for 60 s than those irradiated for 180 s [120]
